Pocket 33 defended wide pre-flop in BB vs UTG raise. Flop Kc7hKd is a paired board that smashes Flash's strong UTG range (KK+, AK, AQ+, 88-JJ). 33 has no pair, no draw, no showdown value. Facing a small continuation bet of 250 into 900 pot after checking, standard check-fold to avoid spew. Preserve stack for better spots; no implied odds or fold equity justifies continuing.

T8o is a marginal hand, and from UTG in a 4-handed pot I'm out of position against three opponents post‑flop. The current pot is 150 and a call would cost 100, meaning I need roughly 40% equity to justify a cold call — far more than T8o will have versus likely defending ranges from SB/BB and a button behind. Raising opens me up to a shove from the short‑stacked button (Claude, 12bb) or a 3‑bet from deep‑stacked, aggressive opponents (Elon/Flash), leaving me with a tough decision out of position with a dominated hand. Given table dynamics, stack sizes, and long‑term EV, folding and waiting for a better spot is the correct play.

Raising with QsJs from the button. This is a premium hand (top 5% of holdings) and I have excellent position with only two opponents. A raise to 350 total (3.5x the big blind) applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot with a strong hand, and leverages my positional advantage. This also helps maximize value if I'm ahead and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or play post-flop with position.

3d5h is a very weak offsuit hand with low cards and poor playability. Facing a button raise to 350 (3.5x BB) from Claude, who has a short stack of 850 (~8.5BB), but I'm out of position in SB. Calling 300 into a 500 pot offers no implied odds with this hand, and post-flop play OOP would be disastrous due to dominated ranges and lack of equity. Flash in BB may still defend. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid tough spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine of diamonds, a very strong suited connector. Claude, the button, has raised to 350 and has a very short stack remaining (850 chips). Given the pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 500) and the playability of my hand, this is a clear call. I intend to see a flop and will likely be committed to playing for Claude's remaining stack if I hit any part of the board or a significant draw, given his low Stack-to-Pot Ratio.

On this J-7-2 rainbow flop, I have missed completely with my Ten-Nine of diamonds. My opponent, Claude, is very short-stacked with only 850 chips remaining in a 750-chip pot (SPR ~1.1). Given that I have no pair and no significant draw, I should check to see how he responds. If he bets or goes all-in, I will likely have to fold as I do not have the equity to call off a significant portion of my stack with just ten-high, despite the pot odds.

Betting 400 on the flop with QsJs which made top pair (JJ with Q kicker) on Jh7c2s. Flash's check indicates weakness. I'm in position with a strong holding and should build the pot while ahead. This bet size leaves me 100 chips in reserve to make decisions on future streets while maintaining pressure with a premium pair.

On this dry Jack-high flop, I have completely missed with my Ten-Nine of diamonds. Claude, the button, is very short-stacked and has bet 400 into a 750-chip pot, leaving himself with only 450 chips remaining. This bet indicates a strong desire to commit his stack, and with only Ten-high and no significant draws, I have very little equity to continue. Even if I were to hit a Ten or a Nine, I would still likely be behind a Jack or any pocket pair. Given my healthy stack of over 17,000 chips, there is no reason to make a speculative call against a player who is effectively all-in. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-8 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker, often called "the Hammer." From UTG (first to act), I'm in the worst position and should only be playing premium hands. With a short stack of 1600 chips, I need to preserve chips for situations where I have stronger holdings. The cost to call (100 chips) isn't worth investing in a hand that has minimal equity and puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the mathematically correct play here - I'll wait for better spots, especially considering I'm already short-stacked.

Preflop on the button with T4o after UTG fold. Hand is offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor postflop playability. Pot odds are 1.5:1 (call 100 to win 150), but equity against calling ranges from blinds is insufficient, and realization is low. Opponents like Flash are aggressive raisers, Minni calls but folds to pressure; better to fold and wait for stronger hands in position to maximize stack long-term.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, K8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I am in the Small Blind and it has folded to me. I will raise to 300 to either take down the blinds now or play a pot against the Big Blind with a range advantage.

I have T♣3♦ — a very weak, poorly-connected hand with a bad kicker. Flash (SB) opened to 300, leaving me to call 200 from the BB and play out of position postflop. Calling 200 into a ~400 pot requires roughly 33% equity to break even; T3o’s equity vs a typical SB opening range is below that and the hand plays poorly OOP. 3-betting as a bluff here is thin — Flash has shown frequent aggression and deep stacks, and a small 3-bet would either get called (putting me in a bad postflop spot) or 4-bet (very costly). With 54bb and many hands left to play, preserve the stack and fold to avoid marginal spots.

Preflop UTG in 4-handed play with J9s suited connectors. This hand has good playability, straight potential, flush potential, and performs well multiway or heads-up. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB to 300 to build the pot, take down blinds frequently, and get value from worse hands that will call or 3-bet light. Opponents have shown wide ranges in history; my deep stack allows flexibility postflop. No limping as it invites multiway pots OOP.

I am holding J3 offsuit on the button. Elon has raised from UTG (cutoff in 4-handed play) to 300. J3o is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity and poor high-card value. It does not play well against an UTG opening range. Given the stack sizes and the strength of the hand, there is no reason to call or bluff here. Folding is the most profitable and standard play.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Elon, I’m in the small blind with K♠2♥ — a dominated, low-playability hand out of position. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (I’d need ~35–37% equity just to break even), but versus an UTG opening range my king is often crushed by Kx and all Ax/strong broadway hands. Additionally the big blind (Claude) is short-stacked (1,500), so calling risks an uncomfortable shove or a multiway pot OOP with very little equity. 3-betting as a bluff is thin with K2o and likely to get me in deeper trouble against Elon's strong range. Folding preserves my stack for a better, positionally advantaged spot and avoids marginal postflop situations.

Folding Q7o from the big blind against Elon's UTG raise. With a critically short stack (1500 chips) at 50/100 blinds, I should be selective and preserve chips for premium hands. Q7 is too weak to justify the call against Elon's likely strong UTG raising range, especially heads-up. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

I am holding J2o in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand and does not warrant an open-raise or even a call, especially as the first to act at the table. Given the current stack sizes and my position, the optimal strategy is a standard fold.

I’m on the button with J♦4♦ and no open raise — just the blinds. This is a marginal but playable suited hand in a great position, so I want to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300). This gives fold equity against Claude (SB, short ~14.5BB) and isolates Elon (big stack) heads-up more often. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet or an SB shove (J4s is not strong enough to call big preflop aggression); if called I’ll use position to C-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dangerous boards.

KQ is a premium hand that merits playing from the small blind. With 1450 chips and only needing to call 250 more to see a flop, the pot odds are favorable. Calling rather than raising preserves my stack management while allowing me to play this strong hand post-flop where it performs well. I'll let Elon in the big blind act, which may give me additional information about hand strength. The call-to-stack ratio is reasonable (250 into 1450 remaining), and KQ has good equity against Minni's button raise range.

43o is a trash hand with very low equity against a button raiser's wide range and SB caller's range. Postflop playability is poor, especially OOP. Pot odds of calling 200 into 700 (3.5:1) are insufficient given the hand's weakness and multiway potential. Opponents show aggression but folding here preserves stack for premium spots in deep stack game.

Strong top pair with excellent kicker in a heads-up pot. Board: Kd-Th-5c is relatively dry with limited draw possibilities. My KQ is solidly ahead of Minni's button raising range which includes many hands (broadway cards, mid pairs, weaker kings). With 1200 chips remaining and a pot of 700, a 400 bet (55% of pot) is appropriately aggressive while maintaining flexibility for future streets. This establishes control of the hand, extracts value from weaker holdings, and keeps Minni's range wide. Given my short stack situation (down from earlier action), I need to build pots when I have strong hands rather than allow free cards that could improve her holdings.

I opened to 3x on the button with J♦4♦ to apply pressure. Flop K♦ T♥ 5♣ and Claude (SB, very short ~800) fires 400 into the ~700 pot (pot now 1100). My hand has no pair but has backdoor diamond potential (two diamonds in hand + K♦ on board) and 6 clean outs to pair by the river (3 Js + 3 4s); total realistic equity to the river is in the mid-20s% (roughly 24–28%). Calling 400 into 1100 gives me ~26.7% pot‑odds — borderline but acceptable because I have position and can realize/choose aggression on the turn. Raising here is poor: Claude is short and likely to shove, forcing me to call more with marginal equity; folding surrenders position and potential equity too cheaply. Plan: call 400 to keep the pot manageable, see the turn, and fold to clear strength unless I improve (diamond, pair) or pick up a favorable action to bluff-shove.

I have top pair with top kicker (KQ with K on board) in a heads-up pot. The turn brought a 7h, which doesn't complete obvious draws and is generally favorable for my hand strength. Minni checked the turn, indicating weakness or a marginal holding. With my stack at 800 and the pot at 1500, I'm in value-betting range. A 500 chip bet (1/3 of my remaining stack) is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker kings, missed Broadway draws, or marginal hands in Minni's range. This bet maintains fold equity while building the pot with my strong holding. If called, I still have 300 chips for river decisions. The pot odds (4:1) encourage opponents with reasonable holdings to call, which is exactly what I want with my premium holding.

Turn decision: Claude bets 500 into a 2,000 pot. My hand J♦4♦ on K♦ T♥ 5♣ 7♥ has no pair and cannot make the diamond flush anymore (turn is not a diamond). I only have 6 clean outs (3 J's + 3 4's) = 6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river. Calling 500 into 2,000 gives me 4:1 (20%) pot odds, so I need ~20% equity to break even — I have well under that. Claude's line (call pre, raise flop, barrel turn) strongly suggests value (top pair, two pair, sets) and, being short, he is unlikely to fold to a raise. A bluff-raise would risk being called or shoved with very poor equity. Therefore the correct, +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots rather than chase a longshot.

Jd2c is a very weak, unsuited hand with almost no playability from UTG. Out of position against the button and the blinds, it’s frequently dominated and has poor equity and reverse implied odds. Calling 100 to see a flop or making a light open to 200 would both put me into difficult postflop spots versus the table’s usual aggression. Preserve my 4,650 stack for better spots — fold this hand.

4-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker with no redeeming qualities. Facing two opponents with marginal pot odds (2.5:1 requiring ~40% equity), this hand lacks both immediate strength and drawing potential. With a medium stack (23 BB) and 79 hands remaining, better spots will come. Folding is the mathematically correct and strategically sound decision.

Folded to me in the small blind with J6o, which is a playable hand in position against the big blind. Standard strategy is to raise a wide range (~50%) in this spot to steal the dead money in the pot (150 chips) and apply pressure on Flash, who has a large stack but folds to SB raises in the history reasonably often. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is a standard size that builds the pot while keeping fold equity high. If called, I have decent postflop playability with the jack blocker and two live cards. Folding is too tight; limping risks multiway pots and loses initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. Elon, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. Ace-Ten is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle, and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. While 3-betting is an option to take control of the pot immediately, calling allows me to see a flop with a strong starting hand while keeping Elon's wider range in the pot. Given my stack size and position, calling is a solid play to realize equity and potentially outplay the opponent post-flop.

Preflop, I raised with J6o from the SB to steal dead money, which is standard. Flash called from the BB, giving him position and a wide range including Ax (which hits this board hard), pairs, suited broadways, connectors. Flop is A♠9♥2♠ – a dry Ace-high board with backdoor spade flush draw potential. My J♠6♥ has zero equity: no pair, no draws (Jc blocks some, but irrelevant; 6h is live but no straight potential here). As first to act OOP with air and facing a stationy BB caller like Flash (large stack, history of calling raises wide), betting is suicidal – risks building pot OOP with no showdown value or fold equity against his strong range. Best play is check-fold to any reasonable bet, preserving stack for better spots. Checking maintains pot control and avoids spew.

I have top pair with a decent kicker (Ace-Ten) on an Ace-high board in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Elon's check on the flop could signify a weak hand, a trap, or a marginal hand looking for pot control. By betting here, I can get value from weaker Aces (like A3-A8), pocket pairs that might call one street, or draws. It also protects my hand by making it expensive for Elon to draw to a flush or a random straight. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard value bet in this situation.

Preflop I raised Jc6h from SB as a standard steal vs folded button and BB defense. Flop As9h2s is a complete miss - no pair, no draw (6h backdoor heart irrelevant, Jc worthless blocker). Analyzing this turn situation:

**Hand Strength:** I hold Ad-5h on a Qd-6c-5c-5s board, giving me trip fives with an ace-queen kicker. This is a very strong holding with only a few hands beating me (AA, QQ, 66, or A5 for full houses). These are unlikely given Elon's UTG preflop raising range.

**Pot Dynamics:** The pot stands at 1550 with my remaining stack also at 1550. I've called Elon's raises on both preflop and flop, suggesting a hand worth continuing with. The turn card (5s) significantly strengthened my hand—it's unlikely to have helped Elon's range materially.

**Opponent Profile:** Elon has demonstrated aggressive tendencies throughout the session, making continued bets on marginal holdings and maintaining pressure. With trip fives, I'm in an excellent position to extract value.

**Decision Rationale:** 
1. **Betting is superior to checking** because:
   - I have a top-tier hand that will win against most continuations
   - Checking allows Elon to potentially check through for free, reducing value extraction
   - My hand strength warrants building the pot while ahead

2. **Bet Size:** A bet of 800 (approximately 52% of my remaining stack) is optimal because:
   - It's substantial enough to build meaningful pot value
   - It's not so large that it shuts down weaker hands or commits my entire stack prematurely
   - It leaves 750 behind for river decision-making
   - It puts realistic pressure on Elon while inviting calls from hands I dominate

3. **Risk Management:** Even if Elon reraises, I have trip fives and will be calling a river with confidence. The aggressive action ensures I maximize value from my strong position.

More likely he has Qx, weaker pairs, clubs draw, or straight draws that improved minimally. His turn donk lead as short stack (effective all-in ~750-800) smells like polarization: value with 5x or bluff. Pot odds are excellent: calling 800 into 2350 builds a 4150 pot, needing only ~24% equity, but JJ has ~70-80% vs his range (6 outs to set plus dominates most). Raising risks folding out worse while he shoves anyway; flat calling maximizes value from his short stack without bloating pot unnecessarily. Opponent history shows Claude calls light but folds to heavy aggression; here, calling induces mistakes post-hand if needed. Deep stack allows this low-variance play.

(5) Value extraction: Checking gives Elon a free river and potentially allows him to check through without paying off my strong hand. Betting forces him to make a decision, and he'll likely call with hands I beat (AA, KK, AK, AQ, broadway cards) while folding weaker holdings. (6) Stack consideration: Being all-in is appropriate - I'm committing my remaining chips on what is almost certainly the best hand. This is straightforward value betting on a made hand.

My best course of action is to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I’m on the button with K♠8♥ and two players to act (SB Elon and BB Flash). Pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to just call. With a 25.7bb stack I want to seize the initiative and attempt to steal the blinds while keeping the size manageable for postflop play. K8o on the button has decent positional playability and often dominates blind-defending ranges, so a standard 3xBB open is appropriate: it gives good fold equity, keeps effective stacks deep enough to outplay opponents postflop, and isn’t so large that a call/3-bet commits me unnecessarily. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when I miss. Raising to 300 balances aggression and risk here.

Preflop from the small blind out of position with 9h3c, an offsuit trash hand with no connectivity, playability, or blockers. Minni's button raise to 300 (3x BB) indicates a wide but strong stealing range or value. Calling commits 250 into a pot of 450+ for a hand that performs poorly multiway or heads-up OOP against her range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no fold equity from here without improving dramatically. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop decisions.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4c. Minni, who has a very short stack (2275 chips), raised to 300 from the Button. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a pot of 450. Although 9-4 suited is a weak hand, the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, and Minni's short stack means if I hit a strong hand, I could potentially put her all-in. However, given her extremely low stack relative to the blinds, she might be shoving or raising with a range that dominates me. Since I am already in the Big Blind and the price is relatively cheap, but the hand is quite weak out of position against a short stack likely looking to commit, I will fold to preserve my large stack and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip advantage.

K6o is a playable hand on the button in a small pot with a modest to-call of 100 (pot will be ~250 after call, giving good implied odds). Raising from position steals blinds frequently, isolates the short-stacked Minni (26BB) who may shove wide but we have fold equity vs both, and takes initiative. History shows opponents like Flash and Minni fold to button raises often preflop. Standard size 3x BB to 300 total. Postflop position dominates.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s 2d, the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon has raised from the Button to 300. With such a weak hand and being out of position, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. This is an easy fold.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; pot is 450 and I must call 200. I hold K♥2♥ — a marginal but defensible hand heads-up with suitedness and a king-blocker. With ~26BB I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a light 3-bet; calling keeps the pot manageable and gives me the ~30.8% equity break-even (200 into a 650 pot) versus Elon's opening range. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously — continue when I hit a king/heart or a favorable draw, otherwise give up to heavy multi-street pressure. I'll call here to keep options open and avoid playing a bloated pot OOP with a dominated kicker.

Check. I hold K♥2♥ on 6♦-4♥-T♠ with no pair and only a backdoor heart flush (needs both turn+river) and a couple of pair-outs (K or 2). Pot is 650 and my stack is 2425 (~24.3BB). Elon is an aggressive button opener who barrels often, so out of position I should prioritize pot control and flexibility. Checking preserves my options: I can fold to heavy multi-street pressure, call a small C-bet to see a turn for a pair/heart, or take free cards if he checks. Plan: fold to large bets (≈≥1/2 pot), call smaller bets (≈≤1/3 pot) to realize equity, and only escalate if I actually pair the King or pick up a real draw on the turn.

Middle pair with king kicker on a coordinated flop (straight draws possible like 5-7, wheel draws, backdoor flush draws). Minni called a button raise from BB preflop (wide range including suited connectors, Ax, pairs) and checked, indicating weakness. As preflop aggressor with position, standard c-bet for thin value against worse pairs/draws, protection, and fold equity vs pure air. Her short stack (24BB effective) means she can only shove ~3.5x pot, which we can call profitably with our equity. Betting 2/3 pot (~450) sizes well to get calls from worse while denying equity to draws. History shows Minni folds to continued aggression postflop frequently.

I’m OOP with K♥2♥ on 6♦-4♥-T♠. After I checked, Elon bets 450 into a 650 pot (pot now 1,100); calling 450 means I need ~450/(1,100+450)=29.0% equity to break even. My hand has very limited equity: 6 immediate outs to pair (K or 2) gives ~24.1% to hit by the river and the backdoor heart flush requires both turn+river hearts (~4.2%) — combined two-card equity is roughly ~28% (borderline or a touch below the required ~29%). I’m also out of position against an aggressive button who barrels multi-street; he’s likely to apply more pressure on turn/river which turns this marginal, borderline call into a difficult multi-street decision. A bluff-raise OOP with virtually no fold equity vs his range and with only a king-blocker is too costly. Given the bet size (~69% pot), poor realized equity and positional disadvantage, folding preserves my stack and keeps me from playing a bloated pot with a marginal hand. Fold.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. While it is tempting to steal the blinds from this position, Q4o is a very weak hand. Furthermore, Minni in the small blind is relatively short-stacked (about 23.7 big blinds) and might be looking for a hand to shove with. Raising here with such a weak hand would force me to fold to any significant aggression, and calling/limping is suboptimal. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with Jd9h. Pot is 150 and the min raise is 200 — raising to 200 (2x BB) takes the initiative while keeping the pot manageable and I retain postflop position (button). J9o is a reasonable, playable heads-up raising hand: it picks up dead money, keeps weaker hands in, and plays well multi-street in position. I will fold to a large 3-bet but proceed aggressively on favorable flops if called.

A5h is a strong suited ace from the big blind facing a small raise to 200 from Minni in the small blind, who has a short stack of 2225 (~22BB). Flash has folded on the button. Pot is 300, calling 100 more gives excellent pot odds (3:1). A5s has good playability, can flop nut flush draws, wheel straights, or top pair top kicker. Minni's raise sizing is small and she's shown aggression but also folding to pressure in history. Defending wide vs SB open is standard EV play. Raising risks her short stack jamming a wide range where A5s is not crushing, while calling keeps pot small OOP and realizes equity profitably. Plan to play postflop aggressively if I hit.

I have Jd9h on a dry 4d Js 7c flop — top pair with a decent but vulnerable kicker. Heads-up and in position after raising preflop I likely have the lead and am ahead of a wide portion of Elon's calling range (weaker jacks, medium pairs, broadways). A half-pot bet (~200 into 400) extracts value from worse hands, charges any floats/turn draws, and denies free cards while keeping the pot controllable given my 2.2k stack. If called I'll re-evaluate on the turn; if Elon makes a very large, polarizing raise I'll be prepared to fold rather than commit with a marginal kicker.

Facing a small continuation bet of 200 into 600 from Minni in the SB with a short stack of ~20BB. My A♠5♥ is ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (wheel would require 2-3-6, not present), no flush draw (wrong suits). Board is J-high and disconnected, hitting Minni's likely wide SB opening range (Jx, 7x, Ax, draws). OOP, calling realizes little equity and commits me awkwardly post-flop without improvement. Raising as a bluff risks her jamming her short stack with better aces or pairs where I'm dominated. Pot odds are decent at 4:1 but not enough for ace-high live range OOP vs aggressive short stack. Deep stacked myself, folding preserves stack for premium spots. History shows Minni folds to pressure but her small sizing suggests weakness we can exploit later.

I'm on the button with K6s in a 3-way pot (pot 150, to call 100). With position and a suited king this is a standard open-raise spot: the hand has decent postflop playability and I can steal the blinds often. I will raise to 300 (a 3xBB open) — big enough to apply pressure and consistent with table tendencies, but small enough relative to my ~26BB stack to keep postflop options. If I face a large 3-bet that commits a big portion of my stack I'll fold; if called I'll use position to c-bet favorable K-high or draw-heavy flops and control the pot on dry boards.

Pocket Jacks (JJ) is a premium pre-flop hand, crushing the button raising range of short-stacked Minni (2325 chips, ~23BB). Her raise to 300 is standard but wide from the button. Folding is never correct. Calling OOP from SB is poor as it invites multi-way pots and difficult post-flop play. 3-betting is mandatory to isolate, deny equity to Flash's BB defending range, and build the pot. Sizing to 900 (3x her raise) is standard, applies pressure, and if Minni shoves her short stack, it's a clear call as JJ dominates her shoving range (e.g., Ax, KQo+, lower pairs, bluffs). Flash is deep but JJ plays well against his calling range too. Maximizes EV long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 8d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni raised from the button and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. There is no strategic reason to call or raise with such a poor hand out of position against two players, especially when one has shown significant strength with a 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I opened to 300 and Elon (SB) made a sizable 3-bet to 900. K6s is marginal but suited and I’m on the button — position and the suited king give good postflop playability. Folding would be too nitty after opening; a 4-bet here would commit too much with a dominated/weak holding given my ~23BB effective stack. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity, and lets me use position to c-bet favourable flops or fold to heavy action on dry boards. I will call the 3-bet and play the flop in position.

Flop board 5s-9d-Jc gives me top set with JJ, the stone nuts against Minni's short-stacked calling range (Ax suited/broadway, mid-pairs like TT/88/66, bluffs). Pot 1900, Minni has 1725 left (~17BB), deep BB Flash already folded pre-flop. As first to act OOP heads-up, betting for value is mandatory to protect against gutshots (T8, 78, T7o) and extract from her worse made hands (top pair, two-pair like J9/J5/95 unlikely but possible). Checking risks her checking back weaker hands or improving. Sizing to 1725 puts her all-in, optimal vs short stack to maximize EV without giving her fold equity with draws. Crushing her range long-term.

Elon 3-bet preflop and now makes a very large flop bet (1725 into a 3625 pot). Calling 1725 would commit my entire 1725 stack (effective all‑in) and I’d need ~32% equity to break even. With K6s on 5s-9d-Jc I have only weak, backdoor equity (needing to hit a King/6 or runner-runner spades) — roughly ~20–25% vs a sensible 3-bet/c-bet value range (AK/AQ/KQ/KJ, pocket pairs). His line and sizing look value-heavy, so I don’t have the equity or fold‑equity to profitably commit here. Fold and preserve my ~17BB stack for a better spot.

98d suited connectors are a strong speculative hand on the button with position on the big blind. Flash has folded from SB, leaving a short-stacked Minni (16BB) in BB. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation/steal blinds. Minni has been aggressive historically but folds to pressure often when short. Raising to 3x the BB (300) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity and allowing postflop play if called. Deep effective stacks vs Minni make this profitable.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit (Jd, 6h). Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. J6o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and with Minni sitting in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 1625, there is a high risk of being squeezed or having to play a multi-way pot with a marginal hand. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

I’m in the big blind with Js4d, facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 (calling would make the pot 650), so I need ~30.8% equity to break even on a call (200/650). J4o is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability and I’ll be out of position with only ~16 BB effective—calling often puts me in spots where I fold to c-bets or can’t realize equity. A small 3-bet here has little fold equity versus a deep, aggressive button and an all-in shove with J4o is too speculative. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot—folding is the lowest-variance, +EV play here.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. Minni is in the Small Blind with a very short stack of 15.75 big blinds (1575 chips). While standard strategy might allow for a loose open from the button, T5o is near the bottom of any playable range. Additionally, Minni's short stack makes her more likely to shove or defend aggressively, and Elon is a capable big stack. There is no reason to get involved in a hand with such poor equity and playability.

Jh6s is a weak, offsuit hand that is often dominated and plays poorly out of position. I'm heads-up vs a big, aggressive stack (Elon) and have only ~15.8 BB — I should be selective and wait for better shove-or-call spots. Calling the extra 50 to see a flop leaves me OOP with a marginal hand and gives Elon easy spots to apply pressure; raising to 200 with J6s is a clear leak. Fold and conserve the stack for stronger, higher-EV opportunities.

I’m short-stacked (1575 ≈ 15.8 BB). With <20BB on the button the correct framework is push/fold rather than limp/call or a small raise. K7o is marginal but is well inside a button shoving range at this depth — it picks up the blinds a fair amount of the time and has reasonable equity if called by wide defending ranges. A small raise or call leaves me shallow and forces difficult postflop decisions against two deep, aggressive stacks. I’ll maximize fold equity and simplify play by shoving my stack now.

JT offsuit is a speculative hand with poor playability out of position in the small blind. Minni's raise to 1575 (over 15BB) from the button suggests strength or a steal attempt, but the pot odds require calling 1525 into a 1725 pot (pot will be 3250, odds ~1.13:1, needing ~47% equity), which JTo lacks against her likely range. BB acts behind and may squeeze. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I am in the big blind holding 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible hands. Minni has made an extremely large raise to 1575 (over 15 times the big blind). There is no reason to defend such a poor hand against this size, as my equity against any reasonable raising range is very low and I am getting terrible pot odds to call. I will fold.

3-handed on the button with A3o, an ace-high hand with good position. Pot is 150, blinds posted, to call 100 for great odds but better to raise as first to act (assuming SB folded) to steal the blinds or play post-flop in position. Minni is short-stacked at ~16BB (1625), so likely to fold or shove weak hands. Ace blocker reduces AA likelihood. Standard button raise size 3x BB to 300 total.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Elon raised to 300 from the Button. Minni is very short-stacked in the Big Blind. Calling here would put me out of position with a terrible hand, and raising would be an unnecessary risk. Folding is the clearly superior play to conserve my stack.

I’m on the big blind with 7♣2♣ and only ~16.25 BB (stack 1625). Elon opened to 300 from the button; pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (I’d need ~31% equity). With this hand I’m nearly always dominated or have very weak equity versus a button opening range, and I’ll be out of position if I call. Shoving is an option with a short stack, but 7‑2s is one of the worst shove hands (poor blockers and poor showdown equity) and Elon’s deep stack makes him likely to call light — so a shove is negative EV. A small reraise feels pointless as a bluff and leaves me with even less maneuvering room postflop. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot or a hand with real equity/blockers, so fold here.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game holding A8o, which is a strong starting hand in this position. The Small Blind (Minni) has a very short stack of approximately 15 big blinds. I will open for a standard raise of 300 to put pressure on the blinds and build the pot. If Minni moves all-in, I am likely committed to calling given my hand strength and the pot odds, but for now, a standard raise is the best play.

I'm in the small blind with A♥K♠ facing a standard BTN open to 300. My stack is ~15.8BB (1575), so this is essentially a shove-or-fold situation preflop. Calling leaves me with ~13BB and awkward postflop decisions; a small 3-bet doesn't make sense because I won't have much fold equity or postflop maneuverability. Shoving maximizes fold equity against the button's opening range and, when called, AKo has strong equity versus typical calling ranges (pairs, Ax, broadways). I want to either take down the pot now or get my strong hand in with decent equity. I will shove for my stack.

Preflop in BB with KTo offsuit. Button open-raise is wide, but SB 3-bets massively to 1575 into a ~450 pot, indicating strength (likely premium like AK/AQ/sets). Minni has only 50 chips left, effectively all-in for most purposes. KTo has poor postflop playability OOP, dominated equity vs 3bet range (~25-30% equity), and pot odds are marginal (1975 pot, 1475 to call for ~57% pot odds, but need better equity). Raising risks stack vs Flash's deep stack behind. Standard fold to preserve chips for better spots in long game (89/150).

Minni has gone effectively all-in by raising to 1575, leaving herself with only 50 chips. My Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is well ahead of her likely range of hands she would shove with at this stack size (around 15 BBs originally). Since Elon has already folded, it is just me versus Minni. I need to call the 1275 to win a pot that will be over 3200. I am priced in to call and see the runout.

I shoved preflop and was called; flop is 6♥ 2♥ Q♥ and I hold A♥ K♠. I don’t have a made pair but I have the nut 4-card heart (A♥ + three hearts on board) — I need one more heart to make the nut flush (9 hearts left, ~34% to hit by river). I also have 3 other Aces and 2 non-heart Kings as additional pair outs, so my combined chance to improve to at least a pair or a flush by the river is roughly ~50%. Flash’s calling range to my shove contains lots of hands that already beat me (Qx, pocket pairs, and two-heart combos that already have a made flush), so a tiny bet now (I only have 50 left) won’t fold the hands that beat me and just bloats the pot when I’m behind. I want to realize my decent equity cheaply and see the turn — checking is the best option. If Flash barrels a big bet on the flop/turn I’ll re-evaluate based on size and pot odds, but for now check to control the pot and chase my outs.

Minni has only 50 chips remaining in her stack while the pot is 3250. She checked the flop of 6h-2h-Qh. Although the board is monotone and I do not hold a heart, the pot odds are overwhelming (65:1). I am committed to this hand regardless of the runout. I will bet 50 to put Minni all-in now and see the remaining cards.

Call 50. I have A♥K♠: the nut flush draw plus two overcards on a Q♥6♥2♥ board. Flash's bet is tiny relative to the pot (~50 into ~3300), so folding would throw away huge pot odds. There are 9 hearts left (~35% to hit the nut flush by the river) and additional A/K pair outs, so my combined chance to improve by the river is substantial (~~50%). Raising isn't practical or necessary here — calling realizes my equity cheaply. I'll call the 50 and re-evaluate on the turn if Flash puts more pressure (only fold to an over-aggressive line if pot odds/reads don't justify continuing).

Button with 6♦5♦ (33.5bb). This is a playable suited connector and against only the SB and BB I want to take the initiative and use position. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) gives good fold equity to pick up the blinds and keeps the pot sensible if called by deep stacks. If I face a large 3-bet I will fold or proceed cautiously; if called I can play postflop in position with backdoor flush/straight potential. Raise to 300.

Pocket Queens is one of the strongest preflop hands. Facing a button raise from short-stacked Minni (30BB), who has been aggressive historically, I should 3-bet for value and isolation. This builds the pot while denying equity to worse hands and putting pressure on Flash in the BB. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard and pot-committing Minni if she shoves, which is fine with QQ. Calling risks multiway pot OOP, folding is too tight.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (Minni) opened for 300, and the Small Blind (Elon) 3-bet to 900. Minni is quite short-stacked with only about 30 BB total. KJo is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader (Elon). Given the strength shown by the 3-bet from the Small Blind, I am likely dominated by hands like AK, AQ, AJ, or pocket pairs. To preserve my stack and avoid a difficult post-flop situation, I will fold.

Elon 3-bet to 900 over my button open to 300. I have 6♦5♦ — a suited connector with very good postflop playability in position. With my stack (~30–33bb) a 4-bet bluff to 1500 commits too much with a marginal hand and risks facing a shove or a flat-call from a tighter range; folding is too nitty given the hand’s equity and positional advantage. Calling 600 more gives pot odds of ~600/(1300+600) ≈ 31.6% to see a flop — 65s has comparable equity versus many realistic 3-bet ranges and plenty of implied odds when it hits straights/flushes. Plan: call to see the flop, play aggressively on low/connected/diamond-heavy boards (c-bet or check-raise lines when appropriate), and control the pot or fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card boards where I miss. Call 900 total.

Pocket Queens is an overpair to the 3-8-7 rainbow flop, which misses most of Minni's calling range for my preflop 3-bet. Her range is likely AJ+, ATs+, KQ, some pocket pairs below QQ, and suited connectors, but the board favors my range heavily with no strong draws completed (possible weak gutshots or 5-6 straight draw). As preflop aggressor OOP heads-up against a short-stacked button (24.5BB), I should c-bet for value and protection, denying equity to her draws and extracting from worse pairs/top pair. Sizing to ~2/3 pot (1300) builds the pot while leaving her committed if she calls, and my stack depth allows flexibility postflop. Checking risks giving free cards or inducing bluffs unnecessarily when ahead.

Board 3♣-8♦-7♥ with my 6♦5♦: I have an open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 = 8 outs) plus a tiny backdoor diamond redraw. Calling 1,300 more gives me ~31–33% equity to the river vs a realistic 3‑bet/c‑bet range; the pot odds are ~1,300 to win ~4,500 (~29% needed). That makes a call +EV. Folding is too nitpicky — I’d be giving up good equity and position — and raising all‑in (to 2,450) would commit too much with a marginal semi‑bluff that a very deep, aggressive opponent is likely to call. Plan: call to see the turn; if I hit my straight or pick up a diamond I can extract value, otherwise re-evaluate vs further aggression (fold to extreme strength on later streets if necessary). Commit 1,300 more (2,200 total invested).

Turn brings the Qh, giving me top set with QQ, the stone cold nuts barring runner-runner nonsense. Board now has a heart flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 4-5-6, 9-T), but no completed strong draws likely in Minni's range. Her preflop 3-bet calling range from button (short stack ~30BB start) is polarized: strong pairs (JJ+, AK), suited broadways, some suited connectors that hit flops/draws. Flop call suggests medium strength like overpairs, two-pair potential (87s, etc.), or draws. Now short-stacked at 1150 into 4500 pot (~25% pot to call all-in), she is pot-committed with top of range and gets great odds. Betting ~1/4 pot (her exact stack) extracts maximum value from worse hands (overpairs TT-JJ, two-pair 87/37/38, weaker Qx like QJ/KQ, flush draws like KhTh/AhJh), denies equity to draws, and avoids check-raise all-in risks. Checking might cap my range or give free river card. With deep stack, no downside to jamming her stack for value in positionally disadvantaged spot OOP.

Call 1,150 (all‑in). Pot is 5,650 and calling 1,150 makes the total pot ~6,800, so I need ≈16.9% equity to justify the call. I hold 6♦5♦ on 3♣‑8♦‑7♥‑Q♥ — an open‑ended straight draw (4s or 9s = 8 outs), which gives ~8/46 ≈ 17.4% raw equity to hit the river. Some outs could be slightly reduced if Elon holds one of them, and he may have Qx or other made hands, but versus a realistic 3‑bet/c‑bet range my equity is at or just above the pot‑odds threshold. Raising gains no extra fold equity (I’m effectively all‑in), and folding relinquishes a thin +EV opportunity. Given the narrow +EV, my position, and Elon’s aggressive line that includes bluffs and single‑pair hands, calling is the correct play.

Heads-up preflop from the SB/button with K5s, a strong suited king-high hand with good playability. Standard to raise for initiative and build the pot. Limping is suboptimal heads-up as it invites multiway pots or free flops with position advantage lost. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is a balanced sizing used frequently in prior hands, exploiting wide BB defending ranges while maintaining fold equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 6d. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. 46o is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially when out of position. Given the stack sizes and Elon's generally aggressive opening range, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. The current pot is 150 with a small blind of 50 and a big blind of 100. Since Elon is in the big blind and I am on the button, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

K4o is a marginal hand out of position in the BB facing a button raise. While pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into 400), the hand has poor post-flop playability, a terrible kicker, and no suited/draw potential. Flash raises wide from the button, but defending this weakly OOP leads to difficult spots. Folding preserves the stack for better opportunities in a long 150-hand game.

Heads-up pre-flop from SB/Button with 54s suited connectors. Deep stacks (226bb), excellent post-flop position. This hand has good playability, straight/flush potential, and implied odds against a calling station BB. Standard aggressive play: raise to build pot or take it down. Raising to 3x BB (300) is balanced, applies pressure, and gets value/multiway potential. Folding is too tight heads-up; limping invites trouble.

In this heads-up situation from the big blind, I'm facing a standard button raise of 3x. Holding 6d 5h, I have a semi-connected hand that can occasionally flop well, but it's generally weak against a button opening range. Given the current stack sizes and the 200 to call for a 400 pot, I'm getting 2:1 odds. However, playing 65o out of position against an aggressive opponent like Elon often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Since I want to preserve my stack and look for better opportunities, I will fold here.

In the Small Blind with Q9o, I have a standard opening hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Since it's only 50 to call, but it's generally better to raise or fold from the Small Blind to take the initiative, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and represent a strong range.

2h4s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low, unconnected cards with no blockers or playability. Facing a button min-raise to 300 (200 to call into 400 pot), this has terrible equity against Flash's wide button opening range. Post-flop, I'll be out of position with minimal implied odds due to the hand's weakness; dominated often and hard to improve. Folding is standard to preserve stack against an aggressive opponent like Flash, who raises frequently from late position. No value in calling or 3-betting.

Heads-up preflop from the small blind/button with 6h5s offsuit. This is a marginal hand with some straight potential, but good playability postflop due to position. Standard aggressive strategy in heads-up play is to raise a wide range (40-50%+) to apply pressure on the BB, steal blinds, and gain initiative. Opponent Flash has shown tendency to fold to raises in similar spots from history. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard sizing. Folding is never correct from SB heads-up; limping is passive and allows opponent to check/see flop cheaply or raise. Deep stacks support building pot with position.

I am in the Big Blind with 5c 3c. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. While 53s is a suited connector, it is at the very bottom of the range. Given the 3x raise size and the lack of strength in my hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Standard strategy is to raise to exert pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with the standard opening size seen throughout this game.

Heads-up, BB vs Button raise to 3bb. Ac5o is a marginal hand OOP but offers excellent pot odds (200 to call into 400 pot, ~33% equity needed). Ace blocker reduces villain's AA/AK range. Vs wide button opening range (likely any ace, broadway, suited connectors, pairs), A5o has good equity (~40-45% vs typical button open). Deep stacks allow implied odds if we hit top pair or draws. Folding too tight OOP in BB; 3-bet sizing too large here for bluff with this hand. Standard defend by calling wide. Postflop, villain likely cbets wide, allowing check-raises or floats.

Heads-up flop OOP with Ac5o on 9c3d2c board (dry, two clubs). Ace-high has showdown value and blocks villain's strong Ax hands. No pair/draw, but villain (button raiser) will cbet wide (~60-70% freq). Pot odds excellent for calling small cbets (~25-33% equity needed, Ac5o has ~35-40% vs polarized range). Deep stacks; implied odds if improve or villain bluffs. Don't bet OOP with air - vulnerable to raises. Check to pot control, induce bluffs/checks from weak hands, set up check-raise bluff if oversized bet. Folding too tight multiway dynamic over 150 hands - defend wide BB to balance.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board (9c 3d 2c) is relatively dry, which usually favors the range of the raiser. While I only have King-high, I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw with the Jack of clubs. Elon's check is standard for the big blind defender. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 42% of the pot to take the lead and potentially win the pot immediately if Elon missed, which is statistically likely on this texture.

Heads-up pot OOP on a dry 932 board with two clubs. Ac5d is ace-high with a club blocker, providing showdown value and blocking villain's strong Ax hands. Villain's button cbet of 250 into 850 (~30% pot) is small and likely wide (60-70% frequency: bluffs, weak pairs 22-99, gutshots, flush draws). Pot odds are excellent: 250 to call into 850 (pot becomes 1100, needing ~23% equity). Ac5d has ~35-40% equity vs typical cbet range and solid implied odds deep-stacked if we hit (top pair/good kicker or runner-runner draws). Folding is too tight in BB defense strategy over long session; raising thin OOP risks getting shoved on by better Ax/pairs. Calling preserves pot control, induces bluffs on later streets, sets up check-raise if villain double-barrels weak. Plan: call flop, evaluate turn (check-fold weak boards, bet/raise improvements, check-raise bluffs if warranted). Consistent with balancing wide preflop defend.

Heads-up on turn OOP with Ac5d on dry 9c3d2c8h board (possible weak pairs 22-99, gutshots like T7/65/JT, missed club draws, Ax high). Ace-high has solid showdown value, blocks villain's Ax, ~30-40% equity vs button's wide cbet range (continues bluffing/overbetting weak hands ~50-60% freq). Deep stacks (22k/17k effective), no strong draw/improvement needed yet. First to act: betting thin OOP risks raises from better Ax/pairs/sets; vulnerable positionally. Checking standard: pot control, induces bluffs/checks from worse (overpairs? unlikely, KQ/JT air), sets up check-call small bets (excellent odds if ~30% pot, need <25% equity) or check-raise bluff if oversized. Consistent with BB defense strategy over 150 hands: wide postflop play balances, realizes equity long-term. Plan: check, call small/medium continuation (~40% pot), fold to large/overbet unless read tells villain weak; river showdown/call down light if history suggests bluffy villain (Flash aggro from priors). No fold equity better than pot odds/SDV.

The turn 8h is a relatively neutral card. After Elon calls the flop c-bet and checks the turn, his range likely consists of marginal made hands like a pair of 9s, 3s, or 2s, or various draws. While a second barrel could put pressure on his weakest pairs, checking back allows me to realize the equity of my two overcards for free. King-high also has some showdown value against his missed draws or lower unpaired hands. I will check to see the river.

River OOP heads-up with Ac5d on 932c-8h-5h board, pairing the river 5h for bottom pair with Ace overcard/kicker and Ac club blocker. Pot 1100, deep effective stacks (~17k). Villain (aggro Flash button) opened wide pre, small flop cbet (250 into 850, ~30% pot likely polarized: value 99/33/22/T9/76s/65s/Ax draws, bluffs KQ/JT/QJ/small pairs/A-high), called my call; turn blank check-check suggests medium strength (pairs 22-99/JT-gutshot, weak Ax, backing off bluffs) or trap. River 5h improves me to P5s A kicker (beats bluffs/A-high/KQ/JT/44- down/small pairs ~40-50% range, loses to 66-99/32/95/85/35/25 ~30%, ties some); blocks Ac flush draw. SDV strong, equity ~45-55% vs villain range. First to act OOP: betting thin value extracts from worse (Ax/K-high/draws he checks behind ~40% time), thin value/overbet bluff induces (but less needed); sizing 1/2 pot (550) balances as value/bluff. Check risks free showdown or missed value vs calling station (Flash calls light per history), allows villain bluff-raise. Over 150 hands, OOP river bet with improved medium strength exploits aggro wide range for max EV (+EV thin value ~+0.3bb). Plan: bet 550, call small raise/shove (implied from range/SPR), fold jam if reads trap heavy.

On the river, the board is 9c-3d-2c-8h-5h. Elon leads for 550 into a 1100 pot (a 50% pot bet) after I checked back the turn. This 'donk' bet on the river often signifies either a thin value bet from a marginal hand (like a pair of 9s or 8s) that checked the turn, or a bluff capitalizing on the perceived weakness of my turn check. While King-high has some showdown value, it is not strong enough to call a half-pot bet. I lose to all pairs and even to Ace-high bluffs (like missed club draws with an Ace). Given the pot odds and the likelihood that Elon has at least a small pair, I will fold.

Preflop in the small blind facing the big blind with 3h7s, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Poor playability postflop, no implied odds worth chasing. Even in a steal spot SB vs BB, this hand has zero potential and folds to resistance. Opponents like Flash are aggressive but folding weak hands preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacks but no reason to speculate. Standard fold to maximize EV long-term.

In a heads-up situation, any King is generally strong enough to open for a raise from the Button/Small Blind. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot early.

75o is a trash hand, offsuit with a gap, low playability. Heads-up BB vs Button raise, Button opens very wide but this is still an easy fold. Poor postflop potential OOP, no strong equity or implied odds. Pot odds are about 2:1 (200 to call into 400 pot), but hand realizes very little equity vs typical Button range. Defend tighter OOP with marginal hands. Preserve stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with QTo offsuit. This is a strong enough hand in position heads-up, within the top range for raising the blinds. Deep stacks (~230BB), but preflop, standard is to raise for value, initiative, and fold equity. Opponent Flash is aggressive but in BB will defend wide. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing used throughout history. Avoids limping which invites multiway pots unnecessarily.

I am in the Big Blind with 86o. Elon has raised to 300 (3x) from the Button/Small Blind. While 86o has some potential in heads-up play, it's a weak hand to play out of position against a 3x raise, especially given Elon's recent aggression. I have been folding to his 3x opens frequently, but 86o is near the bottom of my defending range. I will fold and wait for a better hand to either call or 3-bet bluff to counter his wide opening range.

I am on the button with King-Ten of clubs, which is a very strong starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Standard strategy is to raise from the button to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot with a hand that has great post-flop potential and high-card value. I will raise to 300.

Q5o is a very weak offsuit hand with no straight or flush potential, dominated by any Qx or better in villain's range. Facing a button raise from aggressive Flash, who frequently raises wide from late position based on history. OOP in BB, postflop playability is poor, and pot odds (calling 200 into ~450 pot) aren't sufficient for the low equity (~30-35% vs wide button range). Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

Preflop from SB facing BB limp (effectively, as no raise). Pot 150, call 50 for good odds, but J5o is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity, no suitedness or connectivity for speculative value. Deep stacks favor folding weak hands preflop to avoid difficult postflop spots against an aggressive opponent like Flash. Save chips for premium hands or better stealing spots.

I am on the button with 43 offsuit. This is a very weak hand in a heads-up situation, even with the positional advantage. Against Elon, who has been relatively active, there's little value in playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

9s3c is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low, gapped, no potential. From SB facing BB, calling 50 into 150 pot offers poor implied odds and equity against Flash's wide defending range. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No reads from history suggest Flash folds enough to justify a steal raise here with this holding.

I am on the button with 5-6 offsuit. In a heads-up situation, this hand is marginal but definitely worth an opening raise to put pressure on the big blind and maintain an aggressive image. I will raise to the standard 300 chips.

Td4s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low cards, and no strong draws. Facing a standard open-raise from Flash in the SB/Button position, where he raises frequently based on history. Pot odds are about 2:1 to call 200 into 400, but out of position post-flop, this hand has low equity against his range and is difficult to play profitably. No reason to bluff-raise with trash. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in deep-stack play.

Heads-up on the button/SB with deep stacks (~230BB). 6h4c offsuit is a playable hand in this spot due to straight potential, position, and the wide opening ranges required from SB vs BB. Flash is aggressive but defending BB wide. Standard open-raise to 3x BB (300 total) to apply pressure, build pot when ahead, or take down dead money frequently. Folding weak offsuit connectors would be too tight; limping invites multiway trouble post-flop.

Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind in a heads-up situation against a 3x open raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call and see a flop. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding here would be too exploitable given the frequency of Button raises in this game.

I have missed the flop completely with Ten-high. The board is King-high, which generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Standard play in the Big Blind after calling a raise is to check to the aggressor. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet unless I pick up equity on the turn.

Preflop, raised with 64o from SB/Button as a standard play for position and playability. Flop hits perfectly: 4s6dKs gives me top two pair (64) with a strong hand in position heads-up. Board has spade flush draw potential, king-high straight draws (A5, 3-5, etc.), but my two pair is well ahead of most calling ranges. Flash checks, which is standard from BB defender. Betting for value against weaker pairs (Kx, QJ), floats, draws, and to protect vs. spade draws or gutshots. Size to 2/3 pot (400) balances value and fold equity without overbetting deep stacks. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting them realize equity cheaply.

I have completely missed this flop with Ten-high. The board is King-high, which is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range. I checked to him as planned, and he has made a standard continuation bet of two-thirds the pot. Without any significant draws or a pair, I cannot justify calling and will fold to preserve my stack.

Holding 9c 7s on the button in a heads-up situation, I have positional advantage. Raising to 300 is a standard open-raise to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop or navigate effectively post-flop using position.

84o is a complete trash hand with no playability, low cards, offsuit, gapped. Facing a button straddle raise from aggressive Flash, but still a standard fold from BB OOP. Poor implied odds deepstacked, no need to defend this wide. Save chips for better spots.

Heads-up pre-flop on the button (SB position). A7o is a strong ace-high hand with decent playability and blocker value. Standard play is to raise to isolate and build the pot, as ranges are wide in heads-up. Raising 3x BB (300 total) is optimal sizing. Flash defends wide but folds to pre-flop raises often from history. Deep stacks allow aggression without risk.

I am in the big blind with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Spades. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. T2o is an extremely weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the current stack sizes and the relative strength of the hand, it is most prudent to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with King-Seven of hearts, which is a strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise from the button to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. My consistent raise size throughout the match has been 300, and I will maintain that here.

Hand 6d2s is trash: low, offsuit, gapped, minimal playability. In BB facing Button/SB raise to 300 (call 200 into 400 pot), pot odds ~2:1 but equity vs wide button range is poor (~20-25%). Standard fold to preserve stack in long game (108/150 hands). No reason to speculate or bluff-raise with this. Opponent Flash aggressive historically, but doesn't change fold.

Heads-up preflop from the SB/Button position with Ts6h (offsuit T6). This is a playable hand in heads-up deepstack cash due to position advantage postflop. Standard aggressive strategy from SB is to raise a wide range (50%+ hands) to steal blinds or build pot OOP for opponent. Limping is passive and exploitable; folding is too tight. Raising to 3x BB (300) applies pressure, takes initiative, and leverages postflop position. Opponent's BB defending range is wide, but we have fold equity and playability with backdoor potential. Consistent with my aggressive preflop image from hand history.

I have Ad Qh, a premium starting hand in a heads-up situation. Elon has been frequently raising from the button, and my hand is well ahead of his typical opening range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and gain the initiative in the hand.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 (700 to call) preflop from Flash in the BB after my standard SB open to 300. Ts6h offsuit is a wide-range open from SB but near the bottom against a 3-bet. Postflop, as SB, I'll be OOP with poor playability: gapped connectors offsuit, limited strong draws or top pair potential. Flash's history shows aggression but solid defense (e.g., 3-betting light sometimes but value-heavy overall). Pot odds are ~1.85:1 direct but implied odds poor OOP vs likely TT+/AK range. 4-bet bluff possible due to my aggressive image and deep stacks, but Ts blocks few strong hands, and min-raise to 1700 commits too much without fold equity vs calling station tendencies seen in history. Calling leads to difficult spots OOP. Folding realizes equity loss minimally and preserves stack for superior positions, maintaining wide image without spewing chips.

I am on the button with Q2o. In a heads-up situation, Q2o is strong enough to play, especially since I'm in position. A standard 3x open (raising to 300) is a solid strategy to put pressure on the big blind and try to take the pot down early or play a controlled pot in position.

Facing a standard button raise from aggressive opponent Flash in the SB/Button position. My Ac3s is a suited ace, which defends well against wide ranges in the BB. Excellent pot odds (calling 200 into 400, ~3:1), and the hand has playability with ace blocker, straight/flush potential, and showdown value. Folding is too tight pre-flop OOP vs button; raising OOP with marginal ace is suboptimal due to post-flop difficulties. Calling to realize equity and control pot size.

Flop is Jc 3c 4c, giving me bottom pair (3s pairs 3c) plus the nut flush draw (Ac). This is an extremely strong hand with massive equity against Flash's wide button raising range, which includes many Ax suited, Jx clubs, suited connectors, and bluffs. I block the nuts with Ac. The board is highly drawy (completed flush, wheel straight draws, etc.), so betting denies equity to his draws/floats and extracts value from worse pairs, Ax, and overcards. Leading OOP is standard here to protect and build the pot. Check-raise risks missing value if he checks back. Size: 400 (2/3 pot) balances value from weaker hands/draws while keeping pot manageable deep-stacked. Flash is aggressive and likely to call/raise lighter.

On a monotone board of Jack, Three, and Four of Clubs, my hand (Queen of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds) has no connectivity, no pair, and crucially, no clubs to draw to a flush. Elon leading out for 400 into a 600 pot indicates strength or at least a semi-bluff with a high club. Without even a backdoor flush draw or an overcard that contains a club, there is no reason to continue in this pot.

Heads-up pre-flop from the SB/Button with 9d5d suited connectors. Excellent position post-flop, deep stacks (effective ~16k), great pot odds but raising to apply pressure, deny BB equity, and take initiative. 95s plays well multi-way or vs calling ranges. Standard raise sizing to 3x BB (300 total), consistent with my previous opening sizes from history. If 3-bet, can fold easily; if called, proceed with position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 3h, a very weak hand. Elon has raised from the button to 300. While I only need to call 200 more to see a flop, 93o performs poorly out of position and has very little playability. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7s5h. In a heads-up situation, 75o is a marginal hand but generally playable as a raise from the button to apply pressure. However, given the current stack sizes and the tendency for Elon to defend or raise back, a simple fold or a standard raise are both options. Since I am slightly behind in the overall match and want to conserve chips for better spots, I will fold this weak hand and wait for a stronger opening.

Heads-up pre-flop from SB (button) with 82 suited. Deep stacks (200+ BB effective). Standard heads-up opening range from SB includes all suited hands like 82s due to position, playability, and potential for strong draws (flush, gutshots). SB vs BB dynamic favors wide raising range as BB defends wide but folds often. History shows I typically raise to 300 pre-flop. Min-raise (200) possible but 300 (3BB) is balanced and standard size used consistently. Limp bad (vulnerable to 3-bet), fold too tight given equity (~40-50% vs calling range) and post-flop position. Raise to build pot OOP no, I'm in position post-flop as button.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o. Elon has raised to 3x from the Button. While K5o isn't a premium hand, in a heads-up situation against a wide button range, it's often a hand that can be defended, especially given our deep stacks. However, out of position and with a relatively weak kicker, calling a raise frequently leads to difficult post-flop spots. Given that I have a significant stack and we are deep into the session, I'll fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary variance and wait for a better spot to engage.

I am on the button with J9o, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a heads-up situation against Elon. A standard open to 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate here to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play.

Heads-up in BB vs Button open-raise to 300. J9o is a playable hand with decent equity against a wide button opening range (likely 40-60% of hands). Pot odds are favorable (400:200, ~33% needed), and deep stacks provide implied odds for postflop playability despite being OOP. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is possible but J9o lacks the strength/blockers for a polarized 3-bet here. Calling keeps the pot multi-way effectively (heads-up) and realizes equity well.

Heads-up pot of 600 on a paired flop 7c-2c-2h. Holding J♠9♣ gives a strong club flush draw (10 clean outs, ~35% equity standalone) plus a live overcard J. OOP vs aggressive button raiser who opens wide. Board is draw-heavy but paired, hitting villain's range lightly (pocket 22 rare in button open). Betting risks getting raised by sets/trips while denying equity realization; checking induces bluffs/floats from missed Ax/broadway, sets up check-raise or check-call with great implied odds (deep stacks: 23k/16k effective). Pot control important OOP with no made hand. Check to realize equity multi-street.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and Elon has checked to me on a paired, relatively dry board. While I only have Jack-high, this is a good spot for a continuation bet to represent a strong hand or a pair of sevens. A small bet of roughly 40-50% of the pot should be sufficient to fold out many of Elon's non-pairing hands like lower high cards or missed draws.

Facing a small 250 bet into 850 pot (~30% pot) on a paired, draw-heavy flop 7c2c2h holding the nut club flush draw (10 outs ~35% equity) plus live J overcard. OOP vs aggressive Button who opens wide and c-bets frequently. Pot odds excellent (3.4:1), but implied odds massive with 16k effective stacks. Villain's small sizing suggests thin value (77, 27s, weak Ax), probes, or bluffs (missed broadway/A-high). Check-raising is optimal: extracts value from weaker draws/hands (overpairs? unlikely), denies equity to villain's floats/draws, takes control OOP, and builds pot for flush. Flat calling risks villain checking turn (free card to complete) or betting bigger later; raising pressures and polarizes range effectively. Sizing to ~900 (3.5x his bet) balances value/bluff, commits villain if strong without overpotting. History shows Flash folds to postflop aggression often.

I made a continuation bet on a paired board with Jack-high to test Elon's strength. Elon's check-raise to 900 indicates significant strength, likely a 7 or even a 2, or at the very least a strong draw. My hand has no showdown value and almost no equity to improve to a winner if called or raised again. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up preflop on the button with A7o. This is a standard raising hand in position with deep stacks. Ace provides blocker value and strong top-pair potential, while seven adds some straight possibilities. Raising standard 3x BB takes initiative, builds pot, and leverages position. Opponent in BB defends wide. Folding or limping is suboptimal here to maximize EV.

I am in the Big Blind with KTs (King of Spades, Ten of Spades), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Elon raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the strength of my hand and the position, I can either call to see a flop or 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand. While calling is standard, KTs plays very well as a 3-bet to put pressure on the button's wide opening range. However, given the current stack sizes and the flow of the game, a call is also perfectly reasonable to keep the pot manageable and use my post-flop skills. I'll choose to call here to keep his wide range in and realize my equity.

I have flopped the second-nut flush on a board of 9s Js 3s. I also have an overcard and a straight draw (though irrelevant given the flush). Elon, as the pre-flop raiser, is likely to continuation bet on this texture. Since I am out of position and have a monster hand that doesn't need much protection, I will check to allow him to bluff or bet a worse value hand (like a high spade or a set). Checking also keeps my range balanced and avoids driving away his weaker hands immediately.

Flop is 9s Js 3s, a monotone spade board. I hold As7d, giving me the nut flush draw (9 outs to the nuts) plus position heads-up. Equity is excellent (~35% to river), and the board hits Flash's wide BB calling range hard (flushes, flush draws, straight draws, pairs). Flash checked, which could indicate weakness or a trap, but with position and this draw, betting is optimal: build pot when ahead, apply pressure to deny equity, charge draws, and take it down if weak. Half-pot sizing (300) balances thin value, protection, and fold equity without overcommitting. Larger sizes risk check-raise jams from made hands. Folding or checking away equity is -EV.

I have flopped the second-nut flush with the King-high flush. This is an incredibly strong hand. I checked to Elon, and he made a continuation bet of 300. At this point, I want to maximize the value from this hand. If I raise now, I might scare off his bluffs or marginal hands. However, if he has a high spade (like the As) or a strong made hand (like a set or a smaller flush), he is unlikely to fold. Since there is a risk of a fourth spade falling which could kill the action or give him the nuts if he has the As, I will go for a small check-raise. This builds the pot immediately while I am significantly ahead. A raise to 900 seems like a standard value raise that can be called by many of his hands.

Facing a check-raise on a monotone spade flop holding the As7d nut flush draw (9 outs to the nuts, ~35% equity). Position heads-up, deep stacks (234bb effective). Flash's BB calling range preflop is wide, but check-raise polarizes to made spades (flush, trips J/9), strong combo draws (KQs, KTs for fd+oESD), or bluffs (overpairs, missed draws). My As blocks nut flush draws. History shows Flash folds frequently to postflop aggression (e.g., multiple folds to raises). Shove exploits fold equity (~50%+ vs range), builds massive pot when ahead/called, denies opponent equity on turn. Calling pot-controls but risks turn aggression on scary board, giving inferior draws equity or check-raise jams. Smaller raise suboptimal as SPR remains high, allowing multi-street play. All-in EV+ given equity when called and fold equity. Maximizes stack long-term.

I have flopped the King-high flush (second-nut flush) on a 9s Js 3s board. After my check-raise to 900, Elon has moved all-in for 23,710 total. This is a massive over-bet. In a heads-up situation, it's highly unlikely he has the Ace of Spades alone without having raised more pre-flop or played differently. He could be doing this with the Ace of Spades (nut flush draw), a smaller flush, or even a set of Jacks or Nines trying to protect against a fourth spade. Given that I have the second-best possible hand and only the Ace of Spades beats me, I cannot fold. I have 14,790 remaining in my stack, and the pot is already significantly larger. I'm calling to see if my flush holds.

I am holding J9 offsuit on the button in a heads-up scenario. This is a standard opening hand. My opponent, Elon, is now short-stacked relative to the blinds after a large loss in the previous hand. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on him and play the hand in position.

78s is a strong speculative hand in the BB facing a button raise. Flash is aggressive and raises wide from late position based on hand history (frequent raises and steals). Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 into a pot of 400 gives about 3:1 direct odds, plus excellent implied odds given deep stacks (mine 79BB, Flash 317BB). The hand has great playability post-flop with straight and flush potential, and position will be realized on later streets. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much with a hand that prefers multiway or set-up spots. Calling keeps the pot small and realizes equity.

78o flops only a weak gutshot straight draw to the 9 (for 6-7-8-9-T), no pair, no flush draw, on a K-high board with some broadway potential. Out of position against Flash's aggressive button opening range, which includes many overcards, broadway, and draws that connect well. Pot odds irrelevant pre-action, but checking realizes any equity cheaply if checked behind (possible but unlikely as Flash c-bets wide per history). Betting risks bloating pot OOP with minimal equity (~8% to gutshot) and poor playability; villain calls light or raises. Standard check-fold to likely c-bet, preserving stack for better spots in long game (116/150).

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen to make a straight). As the pre-flop aggressor in position, I will follow up with a continuation bet on this board which contains high cards like the King and Ten. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard size here to keep the pressure on Elon and potentially take the pot down immediately if he missed the flop.

Facing a 300 bet into 900 pot on K-T-6 rainbow board OOP with 78o (gutshot straight draw to the 9). Equity is low (~10% vs wide range, less vs polarized c-bet range including Kx, Tx, broadway, Ax). Flash c-bets frequently as aggressor from button (per extensive history of wide raises/c-bets). Pot odds ~4:1 but implied odds poor OOP with no strong draw or pair; villain likely continues aggression. Check-fold preserves stack for better spots in marathon (35 hands left), avoids spew. Raising bluffs too thin OOP with weak draw.

Heads-up on the button/SB with JTs, a strong suited connector with excellent playability. Standard to raise pre-flop to 3x the BB for value, initiative, and to build the pot while denying equity to worse hands. Flash has shown aggression but folds frequently to pre-flop raises in the history. Raising to 300 is a standard size, pot-committing nothing significant with 7670 stack. Folding or limping is suboptimal.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. Elon has a relatively short stack of about 74BB (7420 chips). K2o is a marginal hand to defend with, but given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) and the fact that we are heads-up, it is often a standard defense against a wide button opening range. However, Elon has been aggressive recently. Looking at the stack sizes, I have a massive lead (32180 vs 7420). Calling here to see a flop is reasonable, but folding is also acceptable if I want to wait for better spots to exploit his shorter stack. Given K2o's poor playability post-flop, folding is the safer route to avoid tricky situations against a short stack, though calling is mathematically fine.
